annotations.label: Countable set

anchor.snippet: a set is countable if either it is finite or it can be made in one to one correspondence

annotations.note: `Set.Countable s` (and the type-level `Countable`) is exactly the notion of a countable set, via an injection into ℕ.

annotations.label: Countably infinite set

anchor.snippet: A countable set that is not finite is said to be countably infinite

annotations.note: `Denumerable` is a type constructively in bijection with ℕ, i.e. countably infinite (and `nonempty_denumerable_iff` shows this equals Countable ∧ Infinite).

annotations.label: Existence of uncountable sets

anchor.snippet: who proved the existence of uncountable sets , that is, sets that are not countable

annotations.note: Cantor's diagonal `Function.cantor_surjective` proves no surjection α → Set α exists, witnessing uncountable types (also `Uncountable ℝ`).

annotations.label: Countable set (equivalent definitions)

anchor.snippet: is countable if

annotations.note: `countable_iff_exists_injective` characterizes countability by an injection into ℕ.

annotations.label: Definitions are equivalent

anchor.snippet: All of these definitions are equivalent

annotations.note: The injection/surjection characterizations are proved equivalent via `countable_iff_exists_injective` and `countable_iff_exists_surjective`.

annotations.label: Countably infinite set

anchor.snippet: is countably infinite if

annotations.note: `Denumerable` captures countably infinite as a constructive bijection with ℕ.

annotations.label: Uncountable set

anchor.snippet: A set is uncountable if it is not countable

annotations.note: `Uncountable` is defined as `¬Countable α` (`uncountable_iff_not_countable`).

annotations.label: Roster form

anchor.snippet: called roster form

annotations.note: Roster/list notation for sets is informal and has no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Infinite set
annotations.note: `Infinite` (and `Set.Infinite`) formalizes the notion of an infinite set/type.

anchor.snippet: Some sets are infinite

annotations.label: Integers and even integers in correspondence

anchor.snippet: for every integer, there is a distinct even integer

annotations.note: This specific illustrative bijection between ℤ and the even integers is not stated as a named declaration in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: for every integer, there is a distinct even integer

annotations.label: Bijection / one-to-one correspondence

anchor.snippet: which is a function that maps between two sets such that each element of each set corresponds to a single element in the other set

annotations.note: `Equiv` (and `Function.Bijective`) formalizes a one-to-one correspondence between two types.

anchors.snippet: which is a function that maps between two sets such that each element of each set corresponds to a single element in the other set

annotations.label: Same cardinality

anchor.snippet: two sets are of the same size if and only if there is a bijection between them

annotations.note: `Cardinal.eq` states `#α = #β ↔ Nonempty (α ≃ β)`, i.e. equal cardinality iff a bijection exists.

anchors.snippet: two sets are of the same size if and only if there is a bijection between them

annotations.label: Countably infinite (naming)

anchor.snippet: We call all sets that are in one-to-one correspondence with the integers countably infinite

annotations.note: `Denumerable` is exactly the typeclass for types in bijection with ℕ.

anchors.snippet: We call all sets that are in one-to-one correspondence with the integers countably infinite

annotations.label: Not all infinite sets are countably infinite

anchor.snippet: not all infinite sets are countably infinite

annotations.note: ℝ is infinite yet `Cardinal.not_countable_real`/`Uncountable ℝ` shows it is not countable, witnessing the claim.

annotations.label: Real numbers are uncountable

anchor.snippet: The set of real numbers has a greater cardinality than the set of natural numbers

annotations.note: `Cardinal.mk_real` gives `#ℝ = 𝔠` and `Cardinal.not_countable_real` that ℝ exceeds ℵ₀.

annotations.label: Countable set (formal)

anchor.snippet: is countable if there exists a bijection between

annotations.note: Countability via an injection (bijection onto a subset of ℕ) is `countable_iff_exists_injective`.

anchors.snippet: is countable if there exists a bijection between

annotations.label: Finite sets are countable

anchor.snippet: Similarly we can show all finite sets to be countable

annotations.note: `Set.Finite.countable` (and instance `Finite.to_countable`) shows every finite set/type is countable.

anchors.snippet: Similarly we can show all finite sets to be countable

annotations.label: Countably infinite set (formal)

anchor.snippet: is countably infinite if there is a bijection between

annotations.note: `Denumerable` is the formal bijection-with-ℕ notion of countably infinite.

anchors.snippet: is countably infinite if there is a bijection between

annotations.label: Positive integers and even integers are countably infinite

anchor.snippet: We can show these sets are countably infinite by exhibiting a bijection to the natural numbers

annotations.note: `Denumerable ℕ+` gives positive integers countably infinite, but the even-integers example set is not a separately named declaration.

anchors.snippet: We can show these sets are countably infinite by exhibiting a bijection to the natural numbers

annotations.label: Countable iff countably infinite or finite

anchor.snippet: Every countably infinite set is countable, and every infinite countable set is countably infinite

annotations.note: `Set.countable_infinite_iff_nonempty_denumerable` proves an infinite countable set is denumerable (countably infinite).

anchors.snippet: Every countably infinite set is countable, and every infinite countable set is countably infinite

annotations.label: N×N is countably infinite

anchor.snippet: The set of all ordered pairs of natural numbers

annotations.note: `Denumerable.prod` (and `Nat.pairEquiv : ℕ × ℕ ≃ ℕ`) gives that ℕ × ℕ is countably infinite.

annotations.label: Algebraic numbers are countable

anchor.snippet: the set of algebraic numbers is countable

annotations.note: `Algebraic.countable` shows `{x | IsAlgebraic R x}` is countable for countable R, generalizing the algebraic numbers.

annotations.label: Positive rationals are countable

anchor.snippet: the set of positive rational numbers can easily be one-to-one mapped to the set of natural number pairs

annotations.note: ℚ is countable (`Rat.instDenumerable`), so the positive-rationals subtype is countable by `Subtype.countable`.

annotations.label: Cantor's theorem

anchor.snippet: Cantor's theorem asserts that if

annotations.note: `Function.cantor_surjective` proves no map α → Set α is surjective; `Cardinal.cantor` states `a < 2^a`.

annotations.label: Reals and infinite sequences of naturals are uncountable

anchor.snippet: The set of real numbers is uncountable

annotations.note: `Cardinal.not_countable_real`/`Uncountable ℝ` give the reals uncountable; the ℕ→ℕ sequence case follows from Cantor/cardinality.

annotations.label: Subset of a countable set is countable
annotations.note: `Set.Countable.mono` shows any subset of a countable set is countable.

annotations.label: Cartesian product of finitely many countable sets is countable
annotations.note: `Set.Countable.prod` covers binary products and `countable_univ_pi` covers finite products of countable sets.

annotations.label: Z and Q are countable
annotations.note: `Denumerable.int` and `Rat.instDenumerable` make ℤ and ℚ denumerable, hence countable.

annotations.label: Finite union of countable sets is countable
annotations.note: `Set.Countable.union` handles binary unions; `countable_iUnion` over a `Countable` (hence finite) index covers finite unions.

annotations.label: Union of countably many countable sets is countable
annotations.note: `Set.countable_iUnion` (with `Countable.biUnion`/`sUnion`) shows a countable union of countable sets is countable.

annotations.label: Set of finite-length sequences of naturals is countable
annotations.note: `denumerableList` makes `List α` denumerable for denumerable α, so `List ℕ` (finite sequences of naturals) is countable.

annotations.label: Set of finite subsets of naturals is countable
annotations.note: `Set.countable_setOf_finite_subset` shows the finite subsets of a countable set form a countable set (also `Denumerable (Finset ℕ)`).

annotations.label: Injection/surjection with a countable set
annotations.note: `Function.Injective.countable` and `Function.Surjective.countable` transfer countability along injections/surjections.

annotations.label: The set is uncountable
annotations.note: `Cardinal.cantor` (`a < 2^a`) and `Function.cantor_surjective` give that the power set / 2^ℕ is uncountable.

annotations.label: Minimal standard model is countable

anchor.section: Minimal model of set theory is countable
anchor.snippet: this minimal model is countable

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize minimal standard models of ZF or the Löwenheim–Skolem countable model result.

annotations.label: Contents of the minimal standard model

anchor.section: Minimal model of set theory is countable
anchor.snippet: The minimal standard model includes all the algebraic numbers and all effectively computable transcendental numbers

annotations.note: The structure/contents of the minimal standard model of set theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Total orders on countable sets

anchor.snippet: Countable sets can be totally ordered in various ways

annotations.note: Total orders are formalized as `LinearOrder`, but the informal observation that countable sets admit various total orders is not a stated theorem.

annotations.label: Usual order of rationals is not a list

anchor.snippet: The usual order of rational numbers

annotations.note: Density of an order is formalized as `DenselyOrdered` (ℚ is an instance), but the informal 'not a list / not a well-order' remark is not a named result.

annotations.label: Well order (least element)

anchor.snippet: any subset has a least element

annotations.note: `IsWellOrder` (built on `IsWellFounded`) formalizes a well order, in which every nonempty subset has a least element.
